revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23991619004|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ina-Becken]][[id:Lembah Ina]][[ja:伊那谷]]


[[File:Ina_Valley_Relief_Map,_SRTM.jpg|thumb|170px|Relief map of Ina Valley]]
'''Ina Valley''' (伊那谷, ''Inadani'') refers to the basin running north to south along the {{ill|Tenryū River|en|Tenryū River|qid=Q13667|lt=Tenryū River|1=Tenryū River}} in southern {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}. It is also called '''Ina Basin''' (伊那盆地, ''Inabonchi'') or '''Ina Plain''' (伊那平, ''Inadaira'').

It is one of the "four plains" mentioned in Nagano Prefecture’s song {{ill|Shinano no Kuni|en|Shinano no Kuni|qid=Q5366538|lt=Shinano no Kuni|1=Shinano no Kuni}}. Historically, it was also written as '''Ina''' (伊奈).

== Natural Geography ==
{{Underline|※ For cultural and human geography, refer to {{ill|Minami-Shin Region|ja|南信地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Shin Region|1=Nanshin region|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11407491}}.}}

=== Topography ===
[[File:20150202日本アルプス雪化粧.jpg|thumb|From back to front: {{ill|Northern Alps (Hida Mountains)|ja|北アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Alps (Hida Mountains)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 飛騨山脈|1=Hida Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1072070}}, {{ill|Mount Ontake|en|Mount Ontake|qid=Q1754806|lt=Mount Ontake|1=Mount Ontake}}, {{ill|Central Alps (Kiso Mountains)|ja|中央アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Central Alps (Kiso Mountains)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木曽山脈|1=Kiso Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1072062}}, Ina Valley, {{ill|Ina Mountains|ja|伊那山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina Mountains|1=Ina Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11380892}}, and {{ill|Southern Alps (Akaishi Mountains)|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps (Akaishi Mountains)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈|1=Akaishi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q415350}}]]
The Ina Valley is situated in the {{ill|Tenryū River|en|Tenryū River|qid=Q13667|lt=Tenryū River|1=Tenryū River}} basin, flanked by the {{ill|Kiso Mountains|en|Kiso Mountains|qid=Q1072062|lt=Kiso Mountains|1=Kiso Mountains}} to the west and the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|en|Akaishi Mountains|qid=Q415350|lt=Akaishi Mountains|1=Akaishi Mountains}} to the east. It lies west of the {{ill|Fossa Magna|en|Fossa Magna|qid=Q5356574|lt=Fossa Magna|1=Fossa Magna}}, geologically placing it in southwestern Japan.

* '''Mountain ranges''': {{ill|Kiso Mountains|en|Kiso Mountains|qid=Q1072062|lt=Kiso Mountains|1=Kiso Mountains}}, {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|en|Akaishi Mountains|qid=Q415350|lt=Akaishi Mountains|1=Akaishi Mountains}}, {{ill|Ina Mountains|ja|伊那山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina Mountains|1=Ina Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11380892}}
* '''Notable peaks''': {{ill|Mount Kaikoma|en|Mount Kaikoma|qid=Q3695507|lt=Kaikoma-ga-take|1=Mount Kaikoma}}, {{ill|Mount Kisokoma|en|Mount Kisokoma|qid=Q3695791|lt=Kiso Komagatake|1=Mount Kisokoma}}
* '''River''': {{ill|Tenryū River|en|Tenryū River|qid=Q13667|lt=Tenryū River|1=Tenryū River}}

The Ina Valley contains wide flatlands and is not a traditional valley. These plains feature several terraces, widely recognized as {{ill|Fluvial terrace|en|Fluvial terrace|qid=Q1069618|lt=River terraces|1=Fluvial terrace}} formed by the {{ill|Tenryū River|en|Tenryū River|qid=Q13667|lt=Tenryū River|1=Tenryū River}}, along with contributions from tectonic activity, glacial erosion, and tephra deposits.<ref>Matsushima Nobuyuki: "Terraces of Ina Valley," Geology Report of Southern Ina, No. 5, 1980.</ref> 

Many tributaries flow east-west into the north-south-flowing {{ill|Tenryū River|en|Tenryū River|qid=Q13667|lt=Tenryū River|1=Tenryū River}}. On the terraces of the Central Alps side, rivers create significant {{ill|Tadekiri formations|ja|田切|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadekiri formations|1=Tagiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11576221}}, some exceeding 50 meters in scale, complicating north-south transportation within the valley.

Although the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|en|Chūō Expressway|qid=Q1091276|lt=Chūō Expressway|1=Chūō Expressway}} crosses fan-shaped areas above terraces, older national highways and the single-track {{ill|Iida Line|en|Iida Line|qid=Q771305|lt=Iida Line|1=Iida Line}} navigate steep inclines and sharp curves to avoid {{ill|Tadekiri formations|ja|田切地形|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadekiri formations|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This results in slower transportation. Additionally, the Iida Line between {{ill|Iida Station|en|Iida Station|qid=Q985322|lt=Iida Station|1=Iida Station}} and {{ill|Tatsuno Station (Nagano)|en|Tatsuno Station (Nagano)|qid=Q843973|lt=Tatsuno Station|1=Tatsuno Station (Nagano)}} lacks express trains, though the {{ill|Inaji|en|Inaji|qid=Q10885156|lt=Wide View Inaji|1=Inaji}} operates two daily round trips between {{ill|Iida Station|en|Iida Station|qid=Q985322|lt=Iida Station|1=Iida Station}} and {{ill|Toyohashi Station|en|Toyohashi Station|qid=Q771465|lt=Toyohashi Station|1=Toyohashi Station}} (as of 2014). Bus services dominate transportation to Tokyo and Nagoya.

The valley is divided by the {{ill|Tenryū River|en|Tenryū River|qid=Q13667|lt=Tenryū River|1=Tenryū River}} into the eastern "Ryūtō" and the western "Ryūsei." Approximately 70% of the population resides in Ryūsei.<ref>Natural Disasters in Nagano Prefecture, Kyōdo Publishing, 2012.</ref> Historically, few bridges existed across the Tenryū River, dividing lifestyles. Cultural differences such as cutting pickles diagonally in Ryūtō but straight in Ryūsei persist.<ref>Ichikawa Takeo (1974), ''The Rivers of Shinano: Part 3, Tenryū River.''</ref>

{{Wide image|Mts.Kiso and Ina valley from Mt.Jimbagata 01-3.jpg|800px|Central Alps and Ina Valley from {{ill|Mount Jinbagata|ja|陣馬形山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Jinbagata|1=Mount Jimbagata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q31701375}}. The forest-bordered areas are {{ill|Tadekiri formations|ja|田切地形|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadekiri formations|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.}}

=== Climate ===
* Features a {{ill|Central Highland Climate|ja|中央高地式気候|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Central Highland Climate|1=Central Highland climate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11363832}} typical of inland regions.
* In {{ill|Iida, Nagano|en|Iida, Nagano|qid=Q841129|lt=Iida|1=Iida, Nagano}} and the southern area, weather resembles nearby {{ill|Tōnō|en|Tōnō|qid=Q7862707|lt=Tōnō|1=Tōnō}} in Gifu Prefecture or Yamanashi Prefecture.
* Extreme temperature fluctuations: summer highs often exceed 35°C due to {{ill|Foehn wind|en|Foehn wind|qid=Q12314|lt=Föhn winds|1=Foehn wind}}, while winter lows can reach −15°C due to radiative cooling.
* Prevailing winds are northerly in winter and southerly otherwise. Average wind speed is low, but strong winds occasionally affect the Tenryū River.
* Frequent fog, with some of Japan’s highest annual fog days.
* Surrounded by high mountains to the south, east, and west, it experiences reduced typhoon impacts. However, prolonged rainfall can occur due to low cloud positions.

=== Earthquakes ===
Numerous minor faults exist in the valley, with the significant {{ill|Ina Valley Fault Zone|ja|伊那谷断層帯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina Valley Fault Zone|1=Ina Valley Fault Zone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11380908}} running from Kamiina to {{ill|Iida, Nagano|en|Iida, Nagano|qid=Q841129|lt=Iida City|1=Iida, Nagano}}. The last local earthquake was the 1718 {{ill|Toyama Earthquake|ja|遠山地震|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Earthquake|1=1718 Tōyama earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q88500542}}. Past major earthquakes like the 1707 {{ill|1707 Hōei earthquake|en|1707 Hōei earthquake|qid=Q2001581|lt=Hōei Earthquake|1=1707 Hōei earthquake}} and the 1944 {{ill|Tōnankai earthquakes|en|Tōnankai earthquakes|qid=Q17392370|lt=Tōnankai Earthquake|1=Tōnankai earthquakes}} caused significant damage, and the valley is designated a warning area for future {{ill|Tōkai earthquakes|en|Tōkai earthquakes|qid=Q851560|lt=Tokai Earthquake|1=Tōkai earthquakes}} predictions.

=== Landslide Disasters ===
The fragile geological structure leads to frequent landslides and debris flows. Major disasters include the 1961 {{ill|June Rainy Season Flood|ja|昭和36th year 梅雨前線豪雨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June Rainy Season Flood|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the 1983 {{ill|July Rainy Season Flood|ja|昭和58th year 7月豪雨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July Rainy Season Flood|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where the Chūō Expressway was buried, causing disruptions.

== Municipalities ==
* {{ill|Kamiina Regional Alliance|ja|上伊那広域連合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiina Regional Alliance|1=Kamiina Wide Area Union|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11358461}}
** {{ill|Kamiina District, Nagano|en|Kamiina District, Nagano|qid=Q1155054|lt=Kamiina District|1=Kamiina District, Nagano}}
** {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|qid=Q840888|lt=Ina City|1=Ina, Nagano}}
** {{ill|Komagane, Nagano|en|Komagane, Nagano|qid=Q838684|lt=Komagane City|1=Komagane, Nagano}}
* {{ill|Minami-Shin Regional Alliance|ja|南信州広域連合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Shin Regional Alliance|1=Minami Sinshu Wide Area Union|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11407490}}
** {{ill|Shimoina District, Nagano|en|Shimoina District, Nagano|qid=Q726999|lt=Shimoina District|1=Shimoina District, Nagano}}
** {{ill|Iida, Nagano|en|Iida, Nagano|qid=Q841129|lt=Iida City|1=Iida, Nagano}}

== Related Images ==
<gallery>
File:20150dd3中央構造線.JPG|From left: {{ill|Kiso Mountains|en|Kiso Mountains|qid=Q1072062|lt=Kiso Mountains|1=Kiso Mountains}}, Ina Valley, {{ill|Ina Mountains|ja|伊那山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina Mountains|qq=}}, and the Central Tectonic Line valley, {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈}}.
File:SuwaBonchiWideTaggedM.jpg|View from {{ill|Lake Suwa|en|Lake Suwa|qid=Q1206692|lt=Lake Suwa|1=Lake Suwa}}, where the {{ill|Japan Median Tectonic Line|en|Japan Median Tectonic Line|qid=Q1185053|lt=Median Tectonic Line|1=Japan Median Tectonic Line}} intersects the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|en|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|qid=Q906591|lt=Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|1=Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line}}.
File:InaDani.jpg|Ina Valley viewed from above
File:20080813InaDani&KoshibuKo.JPG|Ina Valley and {{ill|Koshibu Lake|ja|小渋湖|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshibu Lake|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 小渋ダム}} from the air
File:Ina Basin and Akaishi Mountains from Mount Eboshi 2010-10-dd.jpg|Northern Ina Valley and Yoda-kirigawa (center) viewed from {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Kiso)|ja|烏帽子岳 (木曽山脈)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Eboshi (Kiso)|qq=}}
</gallery>
